properties.name: Start Point: St Roch Market
properties.description: Start your tour at the St Roch Market, a historic public market that has been serving the community since 1875. Enjoy a delicious breakfast or grab a coffee before you begin your walk through the Marigny neighborhood.

properties.name: Stop 1:  The Faubourg Marigny Marker
properties.description: This marker at the corner of North Rampart and Franklin sits at the original edge of Marigny's plantation, and it's a good place to study how the neighborhood transitions from the French Quarter. North Rampart was the back-of-town boundary — "the wrong side of the tracks" — in the Creole social geography, where the grid loosens and the buildings become humbler. The corner Creole cottages here, hipped-roof and flush to the banquette, are some of the most characteristic vernacular structures in the city.

properties.name: Stop 2: Shotgum Houses of Dauphine Street
properties.description:
The shotgun houses of Dauphine Street in the Faubourg Marigny represent one of the purest surviving expressions of New Orleans vernacular architecture — a building type that is both climatically ingenious and socially legible. The form is deceptively simple: one room wide (typically 12 to 15 feet), built perpendicular to the street on the long axis of the French colonial arpent lot, with rooms lined up front to back so that every door aligns and a breeze — or, legendarily, a shotgun blast — could pass straight through without obstruction. What makes Dauphine Street remarkable is the density and continuity of the type: block after block of these narrow facades pressed flush to the brick banquette, each one a slight variation on the same theme. Some are shotgun singles, housing one family end to end. Others are double shotguns, mirrored twin units sharing a center party wall, the working-class duplex of the 19th-century South. A handful have been camelbacked — a second story rising at the rear like a hump, added when a family needed more space but couldn't afford to expand the footprint into the tiny rear yard.

The construction is balloon frame or heavy timber, typically cypress, with high ceilings (10 to 12 feet) that allow hot air to stratify above the living zone, louvered wood shutters that admit airflow while blocking the brutal afternoon sun, and a front gallery — the stoop and porch rolled into one — that acts as a transitional space between the public street and the private interior. Raised on brick piers a foot or two above grade, the houses acknowledge the flood-prone ground while also drawing cooler air under the floorboards. On Dauphine, the paint colors have become as much a part of the architecture as the wood itself: deep turquoises, burnt oranges, pale yellows, and electric greens that turn what might read as monotonous repetition into something exuberant and alive — a street that is unmistakably, irreducibly New Orleans.

properties.name: Stop 3: Elysian Fields Avenue
properties.description:
Elysian Fields Avenue cuts diagonally across the Marigny grid like a scar from an older geography — it follows the ridge of a natural levee, one of the few elevated strips of land in this low-lying city, and that accident of topography gave it a civic ambition that the surrounding residential streets never quite matched. The avenue is wide and formal by New Orleans standards, with a narrow neutral ground running its center, flanked by a mix of building types that reflects every era of the neighborhood's history. 

 The grandest structures are the double-gallery Creole townhouses from the mid-19th century: two full stories, the lower gallery supported on brick or stuccoed columns, the upper on slender wooden posts, both extending over the sidewalk to create a shaded arcade that is at once practical and monumental. The proportions are distinctly Creole — wider than they are tall, with shallow-pitched roofs hidden behind parapets, long shuttered openings that blur the boundary between inside and outside, and an overall horizontality that hugs the flat Louisiana ground rather than reaching skyward.

properties.name: Stop 4: JamNola
properties.description: The building is a fine example of the Creole commercial townhouse — brick facade with arched openings, a corbeled brick cornice, and a spatial plan (front hall, main room, rear courtyard) that descends directly from 18th-century French colonial building practice. The museum inside is worth visiting: its exhibits on New Orleans music and culture are excellent, and the building's layered interior spaces are themselves architecturally interesting.

properties.name: Stop 5: North Rampart Street Corridor
properties.description: Walk Rampart a few blocks into the neighborhood. This was historically where the Creole faubourgs met the back-of-town, and the streetscape reflects that edge condition — vernacular cottages, corner commercial buildings, mid-century structures, all pressed together. Look for the narrow service alleys between properties, a holdover from the French colonial arpent lot system, where long thin lots were laid out perpendicular to the river.

properties.name: Stop 6: Dauphine Street, the Quiet Interior
properties.description: Dauphine is the soul of the residential Marigny. The blocks between Frenchmen and Elysian Fields are lined almost continuously with double shotguns and Creole cottages, nearly flush to the brick sidewalks, painted in every shade of the Caribbean spectrum. Walk slowly. Notice the variations on the shotgun form — some have side-hall additions, some have been camelbacked, some retain their original wood siding and louvered shutters. This is one of the most intact working-class 19th-century streetscapes in the country.

properties.name: Stop 7: Washington Square Park
properties.description: Washington Square is the Marigny's central gathering place, anchored by Elysian Fields Avenue — the street Tennessee Williams immortalized. The buildings framing the square on Elysian Fields are among the neighborhood's grandest: two-story Creole townhouses with slender wooden columns supporting upper galleries that extend over the sidewalk, creating a shaded arcade that reads almost Italianate in its rhythm. The park itself has a fine George Washington statue installed by local Freemasons. Sit on a bench under the live oaks and absorb the spatial quality — the way the dense neighborhood fabric opens up around this green room.

properties.name: Stop 8: Frenchmen Street
properties.description: This is where the architecture and the street life fuse. The blocks of Frenchmen from Chartres to N. Peters are lined with 19th-century Creole commercial buildings — ground-floor shops with residential above, modest brick or stuccoed facades, pressed-tin cornices, second-floor balconies cantilevered or supported on slender iron posts over the sidewalk. The buildings step in and out of the street edge, creating a lively, irregular rhythm. In the morning, before the clubs open, you can read the architecture clearly. The Spotted Cat at 623 is a former corner grocery; the Maison at 508 shows the double-width commercial façade type. Look up at the cornices and the ironwork — much of it original.

properties.name: End Point: French Market
properties.description: End your tour at the French Market, a historic open-air market that has been a hub of commerce and culture in New Orleans since the late 18th century. Browse the stalls for local crafts, souvenirs, and delicious food, or simply soak in the vibrant atmosphere of this iconic New Orleans destination.
